From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Sex of an organism that produces ovums

For other uses, see [Female (disambiguation)](/wiki/Female_(disambiguation) "Female (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Venus_symbol.svg/250px-Venus_symbol.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Venus_symbol.svg)

The symbol of the Roman goddess [Venus](/wiki/Venus_(mythology) "Venus (mythology)") is used to represent the female sex in biology.[[1]](#cite_note-Stearn-symbols-1)

An [organism](/wiki/Organism "Organism")'s [sex](/wiki/Sex "Sex") is **female** ([symbol](/wiki/Venus_symbol "Venus symbol"): [♀](/wiki/%E2%99%80 "♀")) if it produces the [ovum](/wiki/Ovum "Ovum") (egg cell), the type of [gamete](/wiki/Gamete "Gamete") (sex cell) that fuses with the [male gamete](/wiki/Male_gamete "Male gamete") (sperm cell) during [sexual reproduction](/wiki/Sexual_reproduction "Sexual reproduction").[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Martin_2015-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

A female has larger gametes than a [male](/wiki/Male "Male"). Females and males are results of the [anisogamous](/wiki/Anisogamous "Anisogamous") [reproduction system](/wiki/Reproductive_system "Reproductive system"), wherein gametes are of different sizes (unlike [isogamy](/wiki/Isogamy "Isogamy") where they are the same size).

In species that have males and females, [sex-determination](/wiki/Sex-determination_system "Sex-determination system") may be based on either [sex chromosomes](/wiki/Sex_chromosomes "Sex chromosomes"), or environmental conditions. Most female [mammals](/wiki/Mammal "Mammal"), including female [humans](/wiki/Human "Human"), have two [X chromosomes](/wiki/X_chromosome "X chromosome"). Characteristics of organisms with a female sex vary between different species, having different [female reproductive systems](/wiki/Female_reproductive_system "Female reproductive system"), with some species showing [characteristics secondary to the reproductive system](/wiki/Secondary_sex_characteristic "Secondary sex characteristic"), as with [mammary glands](/wiki/Mammary_gland "Mammary gland") in mammals.

In humans, the word *female* can also be used to refer to [gender](/wiki/Gender "Gender") in the social sense of [gender role](/wiki/Gender_role "Gender role") or [gender identity](/wiki/Gender_identity "Gender identity").[[5]](#cite_note-palazzani-5)[[6]](#cite_note-merriam1-6)

## Etymology and usage

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/3/36/Beowulf_-_faemnan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Beowulf_-_faemnan.jpg)

"*fæmnan*", an [Old English](/wiki/Old_English "Old English") word for 'female'

The word *female* comes from the Latin *femella*, the diminutive form of *femina*, meaning "[woman](/wiki/Woman "Woman")", by way of the Old French *femelle*.[[7]](#cite_note-Fowler-2015-7) It is not etymologically related to the word *[male](/wiki/Male "Male")*, but in the late 14th century the English spelling was altered to parallel that of *male*.[[7]](#cite_note-Fowler-2015-7)[[8]](#cite_note-Ayers-8) It has been used as both noun and adjective since the 14th century.[[7]](#cite_note-Fowler-2015-7) Originally, from its first appearance in the 1300s, *female* exclusively referred to humans and always indicated that the speaker spoke of a woman or a girl.[[9]](#cite_note-Merriam–Webster-9) A century later, the meaning was expanded to include non-human female organisms.[[9]](#cite_note-Merriam–Webster-9)

For several centuries, using the word *female* as a noun was considered more respectful than calling her a *woman* or a *lady* and was preferred for that reason;[[9]](#cite_note-Merriam–Webster-9) however, by 1895,[[7]](#cite_note-Fowler-2015-7)[[10]](#cite_note-10) the linguistic fashion had changed, and *female* was often considered disparaging, usually on the grounds that it grouped humans with other animals.[[7]](#cite_note-Fowler-2015-7)[[11]](#cite_note-11) In the 21st century, the noun *female* is primarily used to describe non-human animals, to refer to biologically female humans in an impersonal technical context (e.g., "Females were more likely than males to develop an autoimmune disease"), or to impartially include a range of people without reference to age (e.g., *girls*) or social status (e.g., *lady*).[[7]](#cite_note-Fowler-2015-7) As an adjective, *female* is still used in some contexts, particularly when the sex of the person is relevant, such as [*female athletes*](/wiki/Female_athlete "Female athlete") or to distinguish a *[male nurse](/wiki/Male_nurse "Male nurse")* from a female one.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Biological sex is [conceptually distinct](/wiki/Sex_and_gender_distinction "Sex and gender distinction") from [gender](/wiki/Gender "Gender"),[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14) although they are often used interchangeably.[[15]](#cite_note-udry-15)[[16]](#cite_note-haig-16) The adjective *female* can describe a person's sex or [gender identity](/wiki/Gender_identity "Gender identity").[[6]](#cite_note-merriam1-6)

The word can also refer to the [shape of connectors and fasteners](/wiki/Gender_of_connectors_and_fasteners "Gender of connectors and fasteners"), such as screws, electrical pins, and technical equipment. Under this convention, sockets and receptacles are called *female,* and the corresponding plugs *male*.[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)

## Defining characteristics

Females produce [ova](/wiki/Ovum "Ovum"), the usually larger gametes in a [heterogamous](/wiki/Heterogamous "Heterogamous") [reproduction system](/wiki/Reproduction_system "Reproduction system"), while the smaller and usually [motile](/wiki/Motility "Motility") gametes, the [spermatozoa](/wiki/Spermatozoa "Spermatozoa"), are produced by males.[[3]](#cite_note-Martin_2015-3)[[19]](#cite_note-19) Generally, a female cannot [reproduce sexually](/wiki/Sexual_reproduction "Sexual reproduction") without access to the gametes of a male, and vice versa, but in some species females can reproduce by themselves [asexually](/wiki/Asexual_reproduction "Asexual reproduction"), for example via [parthenogenesis](/wiki/Parthenogenesis "Parthenogenesis").[[20]](#cite_note-20)

Patterns of sexual reproduction include:

* [Isogamous](/wiki/Isogamy "Isogamy") species with two or more [mating types](/wiki/Mating_type "Mating type") with gametes of identical form and behavior (but different at the molecular level),
* [Anisogamous](/wiki/Anisogamy "Anisogamy") species with [gametes](/wiki/Gamete "Gamete") of male and female types,
* [Oogamous](/wiki/Oogamy "Oogamy") species, which include humans, in which the female gamete is much larger than the male and has no ability to [move](/wiki/Motion_(physics) "Motion (physics)"). Oogamy is a form of [anisogamy](/wiki/Anisogamy "Anisogamy").[[21]](#cite_note-Kumar-201922-21) There is an argument that this pattern was driven by the physical constraints on the mechanisms by which two gametes get together as required for [sexual reproduction](/wiki/Sexual_reproduction "Sexual reproduction").[[22]](#cite_note-22)

Other than the defining difference in the type of gamete produced, differences between males and females in one lineage cannot always be predicted by differences in another. The concept is not limited to animals; egg cells are produced by [chytrids](/wiki/Chytrid "Chytrid"), [diatoms](/wiki/Diatom "Diatom"), [water moulds](/wiki/Water_mould "Water mould") and [land plants](/wiki/Plant "Plant"), among others. In land plants, *female* and *male* designate not only the egg- and sperm-producing organisms and structures, but also the structures of the [sporophytes](/wiki/Sporophyte "Sporophyte") that give rise to male and [female plants](/wiki/Female_plant "Female plant").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Females across species

Species that are divided into females and males are classified as [gonochoric](/wiki/Gonochorism "Gonochorism") in animals, as [dioecious](/wiki/Dioecy "Dioecy") in [seed plants](/wiki/Seed_plants "Seed plants")[[23]](#cite_note-23) and as [dioicous](/wiki/Dioicous "Dioicous") in [cryptogams](/wiki/Cryptogam "Cryptogam").[[24]](#cite_note-ShawGoffinet-24): 82

In some species, female and hermaphrodite individuals may coexist, a [sexual system](/wiki/Sexual_system "Sexual system") termed [gynodioecy](/wiki/Gynodioecy "Gynodioecy").[[25]](#cite_note-Bachtrog-2014-25) In a few species, female individuals coexist with males and [hermaphrodites](/wiki/Hermaphrodite "Hermaphrodite"); this sexual system is called [trioecy](/wiki/Trioecy "Trioecy"). In *[Thor manningi](/wiki/Thor_manningi "Thor manningi")* (a species of shrimp), females coexist with males and [protandrous hermaphrodites](/wiki/Protandrous_hermaphrodite "Protandrous hermaphrodite").[[26]](#cite_note-26)

### Mammalian female

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Anterior_view_of_human_female_and_male%2C_with_labels_2.png/250px-Anterior_view_of_human_female_and_male%2C_with_labels_2.png)](/wiki/File:Anterior_view_of_human_female_and_male,_with_labels_2.png)

Photograph of an adult female human, with an adult [male](/wiki/Male "Male") for comparison. (Both models have partially shaved body hair to show anatomy, i.e., clean-shaven pubic regions.)

A distinguishing characteristic of the [class](/wiki/Class_(biology) "Class (biology)") *Mammalia* is the presence of [mammary glands](/wiki/Mammary_gland "Mammary gland"). Mammary glands are modified sweat glands that produce milk, which is used to feed the young for some time after birth. Only mammals [produce milk](/wiki/Lactation "Lactation"). Mammary glands are [obvious](/wiki/Breast#Other_suggested_functions "Breast") in [humans](/wiki/Humans "Humans"), because the female human body stores large amounts of fatty tissue near the nipples, resulting in prominent [breasts](/wiki/Breast "Breast"). Mammary glands are present in all mammals, although they are normally redundant in males of the species.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

Most mammalian females have two copies of the [X chromosome](/wiki/X_chromosome "X chromosome"), while males have only one X and one smaller [Y chromosome](/wiki/Y_chromosome "Y chromosome"); some mammals, such as the [platypus](/wiki/Platypus "Platypus"), have different combinations.[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29) One of the female's X chromosomes is [randomly inactivated](/wiki/X-inactivation "X-inactivation") in each cell of placental mammals while the paternally derived X is inactivated in marsupials. In birds and some reptiles, by contrast, it is the female which is [heterozygous](/wiki/Heterozygous "Heterozygous") and carries a Z and a W chromosome while the male carries two Z chromosomes. In mammals, females can have [XXX](/wiki/Trisomy_X "Trisomy X") or [X](/wiki/Turner_syndrome "Turner syndrome").[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-Hake-2021-31)

Mammalian females [bear live young](/wiki/Viviparity "Viviparity"), with the exception of [monotreme](/wiki/Monotreme "Monotreme") females, which lay eggs.[[32]](#cite_note-32) Some non-mammalian species, such as [guppies](/wiki/Guppy "Guppy"), have analogous reproductive structures; and some other non-mammals, such as some [sharks](/wiki/Shark "Shark"), also bear live young.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

Following experiments by French endocrinologist Alfred Jost in the 1940s, it is widely believed that the female is the default sex in mammalian sexual determination. However, this idea was called into question by a 2017 study.[[34]](#cite_note-Johnson_Hopkins_Medicine-34)[[35]](#cite_note-Science-35)

## Sex determination

Main article: [Sex-determination system](/wiki/Sex-determination_system "Sex-determination system")

The sex of a particular organism may be determined by genetic or environmental factors, or may naturally change during the course of an organism's life.[[25]](#cite_note-Bachtrog-2014-25)

### Genetic determination

The sex of most mammals, including humans, is genetically determined by the [XY sex-determination system](/wiki/XY_sex-determination_system "XY sex-determination system") where females have XX (as opposed to XY in males) sex [chromosomes](/wiki/Chromosome "Chromosome"). It is also possible in a variety of species, including humans, to have other [karyotypes](/wiki/Karyotypes "Karyotypes"). During [reproduction](/wiki/Reproduction "Reproduction"), the male contributes either an X sperm or a Y sperm, while the female always contributes an X egg. A Y sperm and an X egg produce a male, while an X sperm and an X egg produce a female. The [ZW sex-determination system](/wiki/ZW_sex-determination_system "ZW sex-determination system"), where females have ZW (as opposed to ZZ in males) sex chromosomes, is found in birds, reptiles and some insects and other organisms.[[25]](#cite_note-Bachtrog-2014-25)

### Environmental determination

The young of some species develop into one sex or the other depending on local environmental conditions, e.g. the sex of crocodilians is influenced by the temperature of their eggs. Other species (such as the [goby](/wiki/Goby "Goby")) can transform, as adults, from one sex to the other in response to local reproductive conditions (such as a brief shortage of males).[[36]](#cite_note-36)

In many [arthropods](/wiki/Arthropod "Arthropod"), sex is determined by infection with [parasitic](/wiki/Parasite "Parasite"), [endosymbiotic](/wiki/Endosymbiont "Endosymbiont") [bacteria](/wiki/Bacterium "Bacterium") of the genus *[Wolbachia](/wiki/Wolbachia "Wolbachia")*. The bacterium can only be transmitted via infected ova, and the presence of the obligate endoparasite may be required for female sexual viability.[[37]](#cite_note-Zimmer-37)

## Evolution

See also: [Evolution of sexual reproduction](/wiki/Evolution_of_sexual_reproduction "Evolution of sexual reproduction") and [Sex § Evolution of sex](/wiki/Sex#Evolution_of_sex "Sex")

The question of how females evolved is mainly a question of why males evolved. The first organisms reproduced asexually, usually via [binary fission](/wiki/Binary_fission "Binary fission"), wherein a [cell](/wiki/Cell_(biology) "Cell (biology)") splits itself in half. From a strict numbers perspective, a species that is half males/half females can produce half the offspring an asexual population can, because only the females are having offspring. Being male can also carry significant costs, such as in flashy sexual displays in animals (such as big antlers or colorful feathers), or needing to produce an outsized amount of pollen as a plant in order to get a chance to fertilize a female. Yet despite the costs of being male, there must be some advantage to the process.[[38]](#cite_note-Togashi-2011-38)

The advantages are explained by the [evolution of anisogamy](/wiki/Anisogamy#Evolution "Anisogamy"), which led to the evolution of male and female function.[[39]](#cite_note-Bachtrog-20142-39) Before the evolution of anisogamy, [mating types](/wiki/Mating_type "Mating type") in a species were [isogamous](/wiki/Isogamy "Isogamy"): the same size and both could move, catalogued only as "+" or "-" types.[[40]](#cite_note-Sawada-40): 216 In anisogamy, the mating cells are called gametes. The female gamete is larger than the male gamete, and usually immotile.[[41]](#cite_note-Kumar-20192-41) Anisogamy remains poorly understood, as there is no fossil record of its emergence. Numerous theories exist as to why anisogamy emerged. Many share a common thread, in that larger female gametes are more likely to survive, and that smaller male gametes are more likely to find other gametes because they can travel faster. Current models often fail to account for why isogamy remains in a few species.[[38]](#cite_note-Togashi-2011-38) Anisogamy appears to have evolved multiple times from isogamy; for example female [Volvocales](/wiki/Volvocales "Volvocales") (a type of green algae) evolved from the plus [mating type](/wiki/Mating_type "Mating type").[[40]](#cite_note-Sawada-40): 222 Although sexual evolution emerged at least 1.2 billion years ago, the lack of anisogamous fossil records make it hard to pinpoint when females evolved.[[42]](#cite_note-PB-2000-42)

Female [sex organs](/wiki/Sex_organ "Sex organ") (genitalia, in animals) have an extreme range of variation among species and even within species. The evolution of female genitalia remains poorly understood compared to male genitalia, reflecting a now-outdated belief that female genitalia are less varied than male genitalia, and thus less useful to study. The difficulty of reaching female genitalia has also complicated their study. New 3D technology has made female genital study simpler. Genitalia evolve very quickly. There are three main hypotheses as to what impacts female genital evolution: lock-and-key (genitals must fit together), [cryptic female choice](/wiki/Cryptic_female_choice "Cryptic female choice") (females affect whether males can fertilize them), and [sexual conflict](/wiki/Sexual_conflict "Sexual conflict") (a sort of sexual arms race). There is also a hypothesis that female genital evolution is the result of [pleiotropy](/wiki/Pleiotropy "Pleiotropy"), i.e. unrelated genes that are affected by environmental conditions like low food also affect genitals. This hypothesis is unlikely to apply to a significant number of species, but [natural selection](/wiki/Natural_selection "Natural selection") in general has some role in female genital evolution.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

## Symbol

Main articles: [Gender symbol](/wiki/Gender_symbol "Gender symbol") and [Venus symbol](/wiki/Venus_symbol "Venus symbol")

The symbol ♀ ([Unicode](/wiki/Unicode "Unicode"): U+2640 [Alt codes](/wiki/Alt_codes "Alt codes"): Alt+12), a circle with a small cross underneath, is commonly used to represent females. [Joseph Justus Scaliger](/wiki/Joseph_Justus_Scaliger "Joseph Justus Scaliger") once speculated that the symbol was associated with [Venus, goddess of beauty](/wiki/Venus_(goddess) "Venus (goddess)"), because it resembles a bronze mirror with a handle,[[44]](#cite_note-44) but modern scholars consider that fanciful, and the most established view is that the female and male symbols derive from contractions in Greek script of the Greek names of the planets Thouros (Mars) and Phosphoros (Venus).[[45]](#cite_note-Stearn-45)[[46]](#cite_note-46)
